> *Your current cash covers about 5.6 months of observed spending—nearly a full six-month reserve.*

## Recommended emergency fund: **$32,400**

Based on the last **12 complete months**, your household spending averaged **$5,400 per month**.

| Reserve level | Calculation | Target |
|---|---:|---:|
| 3 months | $5,400 × 3 | $16,200 |
| 6 months | $5,400 × 6 | **$32,400** |
| Current checking and savings | — | **$30,500** |
| Gap to six months | $32,400 − $30,500 | **$1,900** |

Because your household includes two young children, I recommend the **six-month target**, rather than stopping at three months. You are already **94% funded** and need only **$1,900 more**. At your observed **$900 monthly surplus**, you could close that gap in just over **two months**.

Keep roughly one month of expenses accessible in checking and the remainder in an FDIC-insured high-yield savings account. After reaching **$32,400**, redirect the $900 monthly surplus toward your next established priority instead of continuing to build cash indefinitely.

**What I can’t see:** Only one institution is represented in the cash-flow data, so external cash or income could change this result. No specific external account was identified from the summary available; [connect another account](/domains/new) if your household banks elsewhere.

This is educational financial guidance, not individualized advice from a licensed financial advisor.
